Summary: This study compares the diagnostic performance of machine learning (ML) classifiers based on unenhanced CT radiomics with that of a radiologist in differentiating between benign and malignant cystic renal masses (CRMs). The study found that ML classifiers, particularly k-nearest neighbor (KNN), showed comparable diagnostic efficiency to the radiologist, suggesting their potential as a screening tool for CRMs.

Item1.reason: The authors used the CLEAR checklist and the radiomics quality score (RQS) to evaluate the quality of research.

Item2.reason: The inclusion and exclusion criteria are clearly defined in the Methods section under Ethics approval and case selection.

Item3.reason: Histopathological results obtained from the pathological retrieval systems of the two centers were used as reference standard.

Item4.reason: Data was collected from two centers (Guangzhou and Zhuhai).

Item5.reason: The study uses unenhanced CT, which is a common imaging technique, enhancing the clinical translatability of the data.

Item6.reason: The imaging protocol with acquisition parameters is described in the Computed tomography examinations section.

Item7.reason: The study uses histopathological results as the reference standard, which were obtained from operation and/or biopsy records. The time interval between the diagnostic imaging exams and the outcome measure/reference standard acquisition is appropriate.

Condition1.reason: The study includes segmentation of cystic renal masses (CRMs) on CT images.

Condition2.reason: The segmentation was performed manually by radiologists.

Item8.reason: The segmentation methodology is described in the Mass segmentation and radiomic-feature extraction section. The radiologists were allowed to observe the enhanced CT images to outline the shape and edges of the masses more accurately.

Item9.reason: The segmentation was performed manually by radiologists, so this item is not applicable.

Item10.reason: Segmentation of whole masses was performed by associate chief radiologists (T.L. and L.H.).

Condition3.reason: The study extracts hand-crafted radiomic features using the PyRadiomics package.

Item11.reason: The study mentions the use of 3D Slicer for mass segmentation and calculation of radiomic features. They also mention the filters applied to the original images.

Item12.reason: The study used the PyRadiomics package for feature extraction.

Item13.reason: The study describes the feature types and classes. The extracted features were classified into seven categories as follows: first-order features, two-dimensional features, gray-level co-occurrence matrix, gray-level dependence matrix, gray-level size-zone matrix, gray-level run-length matrix, and neighboring gray tone difference matrix.

Condition4.reason: The study includes tabular data in the form of radiomic features.

Condition5.reason: The study does not use end-to-end deep learning.

Item14.reason: The study used intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) to evaluate consistency across the radiomic features; features with ICCs >0.75 were considered stable and were included in this analysis.

Item15.reason: The least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO) method was applied to select the most suitable radiomic features to develop a radiomic signature.

Item16.reason: The authors do not discuss the appropriateness of dimensionality compared to data size.

Item17.reason: The study does not use end-to-end deep learning, so this item is not applicable.

Item18.reason: Patients from Center 1 were allocated to the training set, and patients from Center 2 were allocated to the validation set for external validation.

Item19.reason: The authors did not explicitly address potential confounding factors.

Item20.reason: The study reports appropriate accuracy metrics such as AUC, accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, PPV, and NPV. Confusion matrix is mentioned.

Item21.reason: The study includes 95% confidence intervals (CIs) for the AUCs obtained using ROC curve analysis.

Item22.reason: Calibration curves were plotted using the “rms” package.

Item23.reason: The study focused on unenhanced CT images and compared the diagnostic effectiveness of ML classifiers with that of manual diagnosis by a radiologist.

Item24.reason: The diagnostic efficiency of the ML classifiers was compared with that of manual diagnosis.

Item25.reason: The study does not compare the ML models with a simple baseline reference model.

Item26.reason: The model is tested on an independent data set that is sampled from the same source as the training set.

Item27.reason: The model is tested with independent data from another institution.

Item28.reason: The datasets generated and/or analyzed during the current study are available on GitHub.

Item29.reason: The source code generated and/or analyzed during the current study are available on GitHub.

Item30.reason: The models generated and/or analyzed during the current study are available on GitHub.
